Task id: T93 (ledgerRefs goals:G4) — ROUND 2 re-dispatch after a reconciled DISAPPROVE.
Branch implement/T93 (HEAD c44882b), worktree /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ab3db2ecbe75a0a06. Work THERE if reachable; else `git switch -c t93-fix implement/T93` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha (copy from git output).
Go repo — gate `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c sh -c '...'`. gofmt your changes.

FOUR prior-round criticisms (all autonomously-fixable; #1 and #2 are load-bearing):

1. REPRODUCED DAEMON PANIC (a real regression THIS task introduced): on a multi-peer concentrator, a runtime AddPath that DEFERS (EADDRNOTAVAIL) appends to m.defs and the PRIMARY peer's probers only (see the comment at internal/bind/multipath.go:2282), but T93's NEW Open per-peer view loop indexes EVERY peer's probers by the m.defs index — so the next Close→Open cycle (engine-driven on Down/Up) that successfully binds the deferred def panics with index-out-of-range at internal/bind/multipath.go:801 (`pp.prober = p.probers[i]`). Repro: 2-peer bind → override m.addPathListen to return syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL → AddPath → Close → Open. FIX within this task: fan the deferred add's durable prober record out to EVERY bound peer (mint a Down prober per peer, mirroring the bound-add fan-out) so all peers' p.probers stay index-aligned with m.defs — OR, at minimum, guard Open's per-peer indexing so a length divergence degrades gracefully (no panic) rather than crashing. ADD a regression test for the 2-peer deferred-add→reopen sequence that panics before your fix and passes after.

2. DEVICE-LEVEL per-peer PSK keying is MUTATION-UNPROVEN (the T84 isolation property this task exists to wire): changing internal/device/device.go:301 to buildScheduler(cfg, ids[0].PSK, ...) for the additional peers, and independently device.go:306 to AddConcentratorPeer(id.Name, ids[0].PSK, ...), both pass the whole suite. The bind tests supply their own factories and the device test only checks names/virt counts. ADD a device-level test that brings up a 2-peer concentrator with KNOWN distinct per-peer psks and asserts each bound peer's prober output verifies under its OWN configured psk and NOT the other peer's — MUST kill BOTH mutants (device.go:301 and :306).

3. NEW lint finding introduced by this diff: staticcheck QF1008 at internal/bind/multipath.go:675 ('could remove embedded field "peerState" from selector' — m.peerState.name → m.name in AddConcentratorPeer). Fix it.

4. Inaccurate doc comment on the new BoundPeerNames accessor (internal/bind/multipath.go ~:2548): it claims peers[0] carries 'the first configured peer's name on a multi-peer concentrator', but NewMultipath hardcodes the primary name to "" (the device test asserts names[0]==""). Correct the comment (or wire ids[0].Name through if that was the intent — but keeping the primary name "" matches the current test, so correcting the comment is the minimal fix).

Preserve: single-peer uapiConfig BYTE-IDENTICAL, existing runtime-path suites pass, -race green. Do NOT fix the base lint redness (D45, out of scope). Mutation-verify your new tests (the panic-repro fails before / passes after; the device PSK test kills both wrong-PSK mutants). Re-run the full gate incl. -race. Commit, return the structured result json {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.

TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

17	// writeConcentratorConfig writes a single-path concentrator config with numPeers WireGuard peers
18	// to a 0600 temp file and loads it. With one peer the top-level psk is the sole authenticator
19	// (no per-peer psk); with more than one, each peer carries its own distinct name + psk (the G4
20	// multi-peer surface). listenPort is the concentrator's WG listen port (also the outer socket
21	// port Open binds on 127.0.0.1).
22	func writeConcentratorConfig(t *testing.T, numPeers int, listenPort int) *config.Config {
23		t.Helper()
24		privRaw, _ := genX25519(t)
25	
26		var peers strings.Builder
27		for i := 0; i < numPeers; i++ {
28			_, pubRaw := genX25519(t)
29			fmt.Fprintf(&peers, "\n[[wireguard.peers]]\npublic_key = \"%s\"\nallowed_ips = [\"10.0.%d.0/24\"]\n",
30				base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(pubRaw), i)
31			if numPeers > 1 {
32				// Multi-peer: each peer needs a unique name and its own distinct psk.
33				fmt.Fprintf(&peers, "name = \"edge-%d\"\npsk = \"%s\"\n", i, randB64Key(t))
34			}
35		}
36	
37		body := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
38	psk = "%s"
39	
40	[[paths]]
41	name = "a"
42	source_addr = "127.0.0.1"
43	
44	[wireguard]
45	private_key = "%s"
46	listen_port = %d
47	%s`, randB64Key(t), base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(privRaw), listenPort, peers.String())
48	
49		path := filepath.Join(t.TempDir(), "concentrator.toml")
50		if err := os.WriteFile(path, []byte(body), 0o600); err != nil {
51			t.Fatalf("write config: %v", err)
52		}
53		if err := os.Chmod(path, 0o600); err != nil {
54			t.Fatalf("chmod config: %v", err)
55		}
56		cfg, err := config.Load(path)
57		if err != nil {
58			t.Fatalf("load config: %v\n%s", err, body)
59		}
60		return cfg
61	}
62	
63	// TestUpTwoPeerConcentratorWiresPerPeerState is the T93 device-level acceptance: a 2-peer
64	// concentrator config brought up through up() yields TWO bound peerStates, each with its own
65	// stable virtual endpoint. The deep per-peer prober/scheduler/psk assertions live in the bind
66	// package (TestConcentratorTwoPeersEachOwnWiring); this pins the device wiring end to end.
67	func TestUpTwoPeerConcentratorWiresPerPeerState(t *testing.T) {
68		cfg := writeConcentratorConfig(t, 2, 53971)
69		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
70	
71		factoryCalls := 0
72		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
73			factoryCalls++
74			return &dnsresolve.FakeResolver{}, nil
75		}
76	
77		tun, err := up(cfg, discardLogger(t), chtun.TUN(), "wanbondtest0", factory)
78		if err != nil {
79			t.Fatalf("up on a 2-peer concentrator failed: %v", err)
80		}
81		defer tun.Close()
82	
83		// A concentrator peer carries no hostname endpoint spec (validate forbids it), so the DNS
84		// resolver is provably never constructed (Q29 inertness).
85		if factoryCalls != 0 {
86			t.Fatalf("resolver factory invoked %d times for a concentrator, want 0", factoryCalls)
87		}
88	
89		names := tun.bind.BoundPeerNames()
90		if len(names) != 2 {
91			t.Fatalf("bound peers = %v, want 2", names)
92		}
93		// The second identity is wired under its configured name; the embedded primary carries the
94		// empty name (peers[0]).
95		if names[1] != "edge-1" {
96			t.Fatalf("second bound peer name = %q, want %q", names[1], "edge-1")
97		}
98		virts := tun.bind.PeerVirtEndpoints()
99		if len(virts) != 2 {
100			t.Fatalf("peer virtual endpoints = %d, want 2", len(virts))
101		}
102		if virts[0] == virts[1] {
103			t.Fatal("the two peers share ONE virtual endpoint (invariant A1: one virt per peer)")
104		}
105	}
106	
107	// TestUpSinglePeerConcentratorOnePeerState is the T93 single-peer no-regression guard: a
108	// single-peer concentrator config yields EXACTLY one peerState (the embedded primary, empty
109	// name) — byte-identical wiring to the pre-G4 single-peer path.
110	func TestUpSinglePeerConcentratorOnePeerState(t *testing.T) {
111		cfg := writeConcentratorConfig(t, 1, 53972)
112		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
113	
114		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
115			return &dnsresolve.FakeResolver{}, nil
116		}
117	
118		tun, err := up(cfg, discardLogger(t), chtun.TUN(), "wanbondtest0", factory)
119		if err != nil {
120			t.Fatalf("up on a single-peer concentrator failed: %v", err)
121		}
122		defer tun.Close()
123	
124		if names := tun.bind.BoundPeerNames(); len(names) != 1 || names[0] != "" {
125			t.Fatalf("bound peers = %v, want exactly one primary (empty name)", names)
126		}
127		if virts := tun.bind.PeerVirtEndpoints(); len(virts) != 1 {
128			t.Fatalf("peer virtual endpoints = %d, want 1", len(virts))
129		}
130	}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"testing"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
7	)
8	
9	// TestPeerStatePerPSKCodecAndReflector is the T84 acceptance: two peerStates built through the
10	// per-peer construction seam (newPeerState) from DISTINCT psks derive independent frame Codecs
11	// and probe Reflectors. One peer's authenticated frame is rejected by the other's Decode
12	// (cross-psk frames are rejected on the MAC), and each Reflector authenticates a probe ONLY
13	// under its own psk. This is the property the concentrator relies on: PSK is a per-peer
14	// attribute now, not a bond-wide singleton, so a frame minted under peer A's key cannot be
15	// mistaken for peer B's.
16	func TestPeerStatePerPSKCodecAndReflector(t *testing.T) {
17		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
18		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
19	
20		// The scheduler/prober collaborators are irrelevant to codec/reflector derivation, so this
21		// exercises the PSK seam in isolation (nil is never dereferenced by newPeerState).
22		peerA := newPeerState("peer-a", pskA, nil, nil, nil)
23		peerB := newPeerState("peer-b", pskB, nil, nil, nil)
24	
25		// --- Codec: a frame encoded under peer A's psk fails peer B's Decode. ---
26		encA, err := peerA.newCodec()
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("peer A encode codec: %v", err)
29		}
30		decA, err := peerA.newCodec()
31		if err != nil {
32			t.Fatalf("peer A decode codec: %v", err)
33		}
34		decB, err := peerB.newCodec()
35		if err != nil {
36			t.Fatalf("peer B decode codec: %v", err)
37		}
38	
39		// A PROBE frame is authenticated (MAC-covered under the peer's authKey). Peer A's own codec
40		// round-trips it back to an authentic probe; peer B's codec, keyed by a DIFFERENT psk, can
41		// NEVER recover it AS an authentic probe — reaching KindProbe requires both the obfuscated
42		// kind byte to survive AND the MAC to verify under authKeyB, which is cryptographically
43		// infeasible for a frame MAC'd under authKeyA. (Decode's exact rejection is ErrMalformed or
44		// ErrAuth depending on how the random nonce garbles the kind byte under the wrong obfKey, so
45		// the deterministic invariant asserted is "never accepted as a probe", not a specific error.)
46		wire, err := encA.Encode(nil, frame.Probe{PathID: 0, ProbeSeq: 7, TimestampNanos: 1234, SessionID: 0xABCD})
47		if err != nil {
48			t.Fatalf("peer A encode: %v", err)
49		}
50		gotA, err := decA.Decode(wire)
51		if err != nil {
52			t.Fatalf("peer A must decode a frame minted under its own psk: %v", err)
53		}
54		if gotA.Kind() != frame.KindProbe {
55			t.Fatalf("peer A decoded its own probe as kind %d, want KindProbe", gotA.Kind())
56		}
57		if gotB, err := decB.Decode(wire); err == nil && gotB.Kind() == frame.KindProbe {
58			t.Fatalf("peer B accepted peer A's authenticated probe under a different psk: %+v", gotB)
59		}
60	
61		// --- Reflector: a probe authenticates (and is reflected) ONLY under its own peer's psk. ---
62		probeA, err := frame.Encode(pskA, frame.Probe{PathID: 0, ProbeSeq: 1, TimestampNanos: 99, SessionID: 0x1})
63		if err != nil {
64			t.Fatalf("encode probe under psk A: %v", err)
65		}
66		if _, err := peerA.reflector.Reflect(probeA); err != nil {
67			t.Fatalf("peer A reflector must authenticate a probe under its own psk: %v", err)
68		}
69		// Under peer B's psk the probe never reflects: it fails the MAC, or (when the wrong obfKey
70		// garbles the kind into an unauthenticated one) decodes to a non-probe the Reflector refuses.
71		if _, err := peerB.reflector.Reflect(probeA); err == nil {
72			t.Fatal("peer B reflector reflected a probe minted under psk A (cross-psk probe must be rejected)")
73		}
74	}

713		}

125	// NewProber builds a Prober for one path. sessionID is this node's per-boot
126	// session identity (see NewSessionID); it is stamped into every probe this Prober
127	// emits to TAG the boot's stream (T38, defect D12). The responder's session-epoch
128	// reset that lets a restarted peer's paths recover is gated on the
129	// responder-contributed challenge this Prober echoes back, not on the sessionID
130	// alone. All Probers of one boot share the same sessionID (it identifies the boot,
131	// not the path). The logger is used (path-tagged) for liveness transition logging.
132	func NewProber(
133		pathName string,
134		pathID uint8,
135		sessionID uint64,
136		psk config.Key,
137		cfg ProberConfig,
138		clock Clock,
139		logger log.Logger,
140	) *Prober {
141		return &Prober{
142			pathName:  pathName,
143			pathID:    pathID,
144			sessionID: sessionID,
145			psk:       psk,
146			clock:     clock,
147			est:       NewEstimator(cfg.LossWindow),
148			live:      NewLiveness(pathName, cfg.Liveness, clock, logger),
149		}
150	}
151	
152	// SendProbe builds and encodes the next authenticated probe frame, stamping it
153	// with the current clock time so the echo yields a round-trip sample. The
154	// returned bytes are ready for the wire.
155	func (p *Prober) SendProbe() ([]byte, error) {
156		p.mu.Lock()
157		defer p.mu.Unlock()
158		probe := frame.Probe{
159			PathID:         p.pathID,
160			ProbeSeq:       p.nextSeq,
161			TimestampNanos: p.clock.Now().UnixNano(),
162			SessionID:      p.sessionID,
163			// Echo back the responder's last-issued challenge (zero until we have seen an
164			// echo) so the responder can verify our liveness and adopt this session.
165			Challenge: p.learnedChallenge,
166		}
167		p.nextSeq++
168		return frame.Encode(p.psk, probe)
169	}
170	
171	// HandleEcho processes one received echo frame. It rejects, without touching
172	// liveness or the RTT estimate: forged/tampered echoes (frame.Decode fails the
173	// HMAC -> ErrAuth), non-probe frames, echoes for another path (ErrPathMismatch),
174	// echoes stamped with a foreign SessionID (ErrSessionMismatch, a stale cross-boot
175	// echo — T38/D12), and replayed/stale ProbeSeq (ErrReplay, defect D4). A genuine echo's ProbeSeq
176	// is folded into the per-path loss estimate whether or not it is fresh (a
177	// duplicate is idempotent, a reorder fills its gap), but only a FRESH echo yields
178	// a new RTT sample and a liveness heartbeat.
179	func (p *Prober) HandleEcho(raw []byte) error {
180		f, err := frame.Decode(p.psk, raw)
181		if err != nil {
182			return err
183		}
184		probe, ok := f.(frame.Probe)
185		if !ok {
186			return fmt.Errorf("telemetry: expected probe echo, got frame kind %d", f.Kind())
187		}
188		if probe.PathID != p.pathID {
189			return ErrPathMismatch
190		}
191		// The SessionID is immutable after construction, so it is read without the lock.
192		// A genuine echo of one of THIS boot's probes always carries our own SessionID
193		// (we stamped it; the responder reflected it verbatim). A mismatch is a stale
194		// cross-boot echo or a replay of one — reject it before it can advance this
195		// boot's high-water or liveness.
196		if probe.SessionID != p.sessionID {
197			return ErrSessionMismatch
198		}
199	
200		p.mu.Lock()
201		defer p.mu.Unlock()
202	
203		// Loss accounting sees every genuine echo of this path: a duplicate re-marks an
204		// already-received slot (idempotent) and a reordered echo fills its gap.
205		p.est.ObserveProbeEcho(probe.ProbeSeq)
206	
207		if !p.guard.Accept(probe.ProbeSeq) {
208			return ErrReplay
209		}
210		// Record the responder's live issued challenge from this FRESH echo so our
211		// subsequent probes prove liveness and (after a restart) get us re-adopted. Only
212		// fresh echoes update it, so a replayed old echo can never roll the learned
213		// challenge backward and stall re-adoption.
214		p.learnedChallenge = probe.Challenge
215		rtt := p.clock.Now().Sub(time.Unix(0, probe.TimestampNanos))
216		if rtt < 0 {
217			// A negative sample can only come from clock skew between send and receive;
218			// clamp rather than poison the EWMA with a negative RTT.
219			rtt = 0
220		}
221		p.est.ObserveRTT(rtt)
222		p.live.RecordEcho()
223		return nil
224	}

226	// Tick advances the liveness state machine against the clock; call it at least
227	// once per probe interval.
228	func (p *Prober) Tick() {
229		p.mu.Lock()
230		defer p.mu.Unlock()
231		p.live.Tick()
232	}
233	
234	// Estimate returns the current per-path quality snapshot.
235	func (p *Prober) Estimate() Estimate {
236		p.mu.Lock()
237		defer p.mu.Unlock()
238		return p.est.Estimate()
239	}

241	// State returns the current liveness verdict.
242	func (p *Prober) State() PathState {
243		p.mu.Lock()
244		defer p.mu.Unlock()
245		return p.live.State()
246	}
247	
248	// PathID returns this path's stable on-wire probe stamp, set at construction and
249	// immutable for the Prober's life (so it is read without the lock). It is the
250	// canonical path identity the peer's Reflector keys anti-replay/session-challenge
251	// on; the bind reconciles a path's DATA-frame id (pathState.id) to it across a
252	// Close->Open cycle so DATA and PROBE agree and a survivor is never renumbered.
253	func (p *Prober) PathID() uint8 { return p.pathID }
254	
255	// reflectorPath is the Reflector's bounded per-path state: exactly one entry per
256	// PathID, so memory is O(paths) with NO retired-session set. It holds the
257	// currently-adopted originator session, its strict-monotonic within-session
258	// anti-replay high-water, and the LIVE issued challenge peers must echo back to
259	// authorize a session-epoch reset.
260	type reflectorPath struct {
261		session   uint64     // the adopted originator SessionID (meaningful once adopted)
262		guard     AntiReplay // ProbeSeq high-water for the currently-adopted session
263		challenge uint64     // issuedChallenge: the live freshness token, rotated on every adoption
264		adopted   bool       // whether any session has been adopted on this path yet
265	}
266	
267	// Reflector is the responder side of the probe exchange. Reflect authenticates an
268	// inbound probe and returns a fresh authenticated ECHO of it (verbatim ProbeSeq /
269	// TimestampNanos / SessionID, IsEcho set, and the path's live issued challenge
270	// stamped into Challenge) so the initiator can compute the round-trip AND learn the
271	// challenge. The responder never manufactures a timestamp, so it needs no
272	// synchronized clock.
273	//
274	// Freshness is RESPONDER-CONTRIBUTED (T38 redesign, defect D12). Each path holds a
275	// random issuedChallenge that the Reflector delivers inside every echo (confidential
276	// + MAC-covered, so only the live PSK-holding peer can read it). A probe authorizes
277	// a session-epoch reset ONLY if it echoes back that live challenge — which a replay
278	// attacker, who never sees the current challenge, can never do. Unpredictability of
279	// a SessionID is therefore NOT treated as freshness: a novel SessionID alone resets

281	// echoed Challenge C on a path whose live state is (session, high-water,
282	// issuedChallenge):
283	//
284	//   - S == the adopted session: strict-monotonic within-session replay rejection
285	//     (D4). A fresh ProbeSeq is accepted and reflected; a stale/replayed one is
286	//     rejected (ErrReplay, and NOT reflected — no echo oracle for a known duplicate).
287	//   - S != the adopted session AND C == issuedChallenge: a genuine peer that received
288	//     our live echo. Adopt S as the new epoch and reset the high-water so its
289	//     seq-from-0 stream is accepted (the D12 restart recovery), then ROTATE
290	//     issuedChallenge so this very adoption probe can never be replayed later to
291	//     re-adopt a superseded session.
292	//   - S != the adopted session AND C != issuedChallenge: EITHER a replay attacker
293	//     (stale/zero challenge — cannot seize the epoch) OR a freshly-restarted peer
294	//     that has not yet learned our challenge. Do NOT adopt/reset, but STILL reflect
295	//     the current issuedChallenge so a genuine peer learns it and is adopted on its
296	//     next probe. A replayed frame is thus harmless: reflected 1:1, never seizing.

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"crypto/rand"
5		"net"

7		"sync"

17	)
18	
19	// fakeDeferredBinder is the injected listen seam (m.deferredListen) the reconcile
20	// tests drive: it returns EADDRNOTAVAIL — the "source_addr not yet assignable" signal
21	// reconcileDeferred reads to keep a path deferred — until arm() is called, after which
22	// it binds a real loopback socket (the address "became assignable"). The returned
23	// socket is adopted by m.paths on a successful promote and closed by m.Close, so the
24	// seam leaks nothing. It is safe for the single-goroutine synchronous test driver.
25	type fakeDeferredBinder struct {
26		mu    sync.Mutex
27		armed bool
28		// devs records the dev argument of every listen() call, in call order, so a test
29		// can assert what the caller's forced-device-bind DECISION threaded through to the
30		// actual bind — the T106 round-2 gap: dev was accepted but never inspected by any
31		// test, so neutering that decision to always "" passed the full suite.
32		devs []string
33	}
34	
35	func (f *fakeDeferredBinder) arm() {
36		f.mu.Lock()
37		f.armed = true
38		f.mu.Unlock()
39	}
40	
41	func (f *fakeDeferredBinder) listen(_ netip.Addr, _ uint16, dev string) (*net.UDPConn, error) {
42		f.mu.Lock()
43		f.devs = append(f.devs, dev)
44		armed := f.armed
45		f.mu.Unlock()
46		if !armed {
47			// Exactly the error a real not-yet-assignable source_addr yields; reconcileDeferred
48			// keeps the path deferred and retries next tick.
49			return nil, syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL
50		}
51		// The address is now assignable: bind a real loopback socket standing in for the
52		// interface that just came up, so the promoted path has a working transport the test
53		// can probe over.
54		return net.ListenUDP("udp", &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.ParseIP("127.0.0.1")})
55	}
56	
57	// TestReconcilePromotesDeferredPathToLive is the T55 core acceptance: a path DEFERRED
58	// at Open because its source_addr was not assignable (EADDRNOTAVAIL) is BOUND and
59	// PROMOTED to a live path by the background reconcile once the address becomes
60	// assignable — brought into m.paths, the scheduler, and its reader — so the scheduler
61	// then selects it, WITHOUT a Close→Open restart. It also asserts the promoted path
62	// reuses its deferred boot prober (id-stamp continuity, T51). The promotion assertion
63	// FAILS on the pre-T55 code, which has no reconcile: a deferred path stays Down forever.
64	func TestReconcilePromotesDeferredPathToLive(t *testing.T) {
65		psk := testKey(t, 0x55)
66		clk := newFakeClock()
67		paths := []config.Path{
68			{Name: "bindable", SourceAddr: netip.MustParseAddr("127.0.0.1")},
69			{Name: "deferred", SourceAddr: netip.MustParseAddr(unassignableSource)},
70		}
71		m, probers, scheduler := newProbingMultipath(t, paths, psk, clk)
72		binder := &fakeDeferredBinder{}
73		m.deferredListen = binder.listen
74	
75		if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
76			t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
77		}
78		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
79	
80		// Precondition (T51): path 1 is deferred, not live.
81		if len(m.paths) != 1 || len(m.deferred) != 1 {
82			t.Fatalf("after Open: paths=%d deferred=%d, want 1 and 1", len(m.paths), len(m.deferred))
83		}
84	
85		// Reconcile while the address is STILL unassignable: nothing is promoted, the path
86		// stays deferred and is retried.
87		m.reconcileDeferred()
88		if len(m.paths) != 1 || len(m.deferred) != 1 {
89			t.Fatalf("reconcile before the address is assignable promoted a path: paths=%d deferred=%d", len(m.paths), len(m.deferred))
90		}

fix(bind,device): fan deferred AddPath prober to every peer; prove per-peer PSK keying (T93 r2)

Round-2 fixes for the four reconciled review findings:

1. Panic regression (index-out-of-range in Open): on a multi-peer
   concentrator a runtime AddPath that DEFERS (EADDRNOTAVAIL) appended the
   deferred def to the SHARED m.defs but grew only the PRIMARY peer's
   probers, leaving a concentrator peer's p.probers short of m.defs. The
   next Close->Open that successfully bound the deferred def then indexed
   that peer's p.probers[i] out of range and panicked at
   `pp.prober = p.probers[i]`. Fan the deferred admission out to EVERY bound
   peer (mint each peer's own Down prober, keyed on its psk), mirroring the
   bound-add fan-out, so every peer's prober slice stays index-aligned with
   m.defs. Add a fail-fast bounds guard in Open as defense-in-depth (a bind
   error, not a crash, on any future divergence). Regression test panics
   before the fix, passes after.

2. Device-level per-peer PSK keying was mutation-unproven. Add
   TestUpTwoPeerConcentratorKeysEachPeerOnItsOwnPSK: brings up a 2-peer
   concentrator with known distinct per-peer psks through the real up()
   wiring and asserts the additional peer is keyed on its OWN psk on both
   the prober (send) and reflector (receive) planes — kills both mutants
   (device.go:301 prober psk, device.go:306 codec/reflector psk). Adds two
   observability accessors (PeerBootProbe, PeerReflect) to reach the seam.

3. staticcheck QF1008: m.peerState.name -> m.name in AddConcentratorPeer.

4. Correct the BoundPeerNames doc comment: NewMultipath always names the
   primary "" (not "the first configured peer's name" on a concentrator).

Single-peer uapiConfig untouched; -race and full suite green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
